title: API Keys & Credentials
lede: Two pages turn iMash from a dashboard into a hub: API Keys let your own software drive iMash through the REST API, and Credentials let iMash run on your own AI provider accounts — your OpenAI, ElevenLabs, or Deepgram keys, even your own S3 bucket for recordings. This guide walks you through both, end to end.

steps.title: Know the difference: API Keys vs. Credentials
steps.content:
iMash has two key-related pages, and they point in opposite directions:

  • API Keys (your code → iMash): secret tokens that identify your account when your own backend, scripts, or third-party tools call the iMash REST API — start calls, read call logs, sync CRM records, and more, without ever logging into the dashboard.
  • Credentials (iMash → your providers): a secure vault where you store your own accounts' keys for voice (TTS), LLM, and transcription providers, plus optional cloud storage. When a provider is keyed here, your agents run on your account and its billing instead of iMash's.
💡 You don't need either page to start using iMash — agents work out of the box on platform-managed providers. Come here when you want to integrate programmatically or bring your own provider accounts.

steps.title: Open the API Keys page
steps.content:
1. In the top navigation bar, click the Credentials section (far right, with the 🔍 key icon).
  2. In the left sidebar, scroll down to the Account group and click API Keys.

You'll see a table of every key on your account, with one row per key:

  • Name — the label you gave the key when you created it.
  • Key Prefix — the first characters of the key, so you can match it to the copy in your code. The full secret is never shown again after creation.
  • Created At — when the key was generated.
  • Last Used — the last time this key authenticated an API request (Never if it hasn't been used yet). Perfect for spotting dead keys.
  • Actions — the red trash icon revokes the key.

images.alt: API Keys page listing keys with name, prefix, created date, and last used time
images.caption: The API Keys page: every key with its name, prefix, creation date, and last-used time.

images.alt: API Keys page listing keys with name, prefix, created date, and last used time
images.caption: The API Keys page: every key with its name, prefix, creation date, and last-used time.

steps.title: Generate a new API key
steps.content:
1. Click + Generate New API Key in the top-right corner.
  2. In the modal, type a clear, descriptive name — for example "Zapier integration" or "Nightly CRM sync" — so you can tell your keys apart months from now.
  3. Click Generate New API Key. (The button stays disabled until you enter a name.)
💡 Create one key per integration. When you retire or rotate one tool, you can revoke just its key without breaking everything else.

images.alt: Generate New API Key modal asking for a descriptive key name
images.caption: Name your key after the integration that will use it.

images.alt: Generate New API Key modal asking for a descriptive key name
images.caption: Name your key after the integration that will use it.

steps.title: Copy the secret key — this is your only chance
steps.content:
The moment the key is generated, iMash shows it once:

  • Key Name — the name you chose.
  • Key Prefix (for identification) — the short public prefix that will appear in the keys table.
  • Secret Key (copy this!) — the full secret, masked by default. Click the eye icon to reveal it and Copy to copy it to your clipboard.
  1. Click Copy and paste the key straight into your integration's secret store or an environment variable.
  2. Click Close.
⚠️ The full key is shown only once. iMash stores just a hashed version — if you lose the key, you can't retrieve it; you'll have to generate a new one. Never paste it into shared documents, chat, front-end code, or mobile apps.

images.alt: API Key Generated modal showing the key name, prefix, and masked secret key with eye and Copy buttons
images.caption: The one-time reveal: use the eye icon to show the secret and Copy to grab it.

images.alt: API Key Generated modal showing the key name, prefix, and masked secret key with eye and Copy buttons
images.caption: The one-time reveal: use the eye icon to show the secret and Copy to grab it.

steps.title: Use the key in your code
steps.content:
Send the key with every request to the iMash REST API — it takes the place of your username and password:

  • Pass it in the Authorization header of your HTTP requests, exactly as shown in the API Reference for each endpoint.
  • Each key acts with your account's full permissions, so treat it like a password.
  • Store it server-side only — environment variables or a secrets manager, never in client-side code.

Once your first request lands, the key's Last Used column starts updating — a quick way to confirm your integration is alive.

steps.title: Revoke a key you no longer need
steps.content:
1. Find the key's row in the table (check Last Used first — Never or an old date usually means it's safe to remove).
  2. Click the red trash icon under Actions.
  3. In the Delete API Key? confirmation, click Delete.
⚠️ Revocation is instant and permanent — any integration still using the key loses access immediately. Switch the integration to a new key before deleting the old one.

images.alt: Delete API Key confirmation dialog with Cancel and Delete buttons
images.caption: The delete confirmation — this action cannot be undone.

images.alt: Delete API Key confirmation dialog with Cancel and Delete buttons
images.caption: The delete confirmation — this action cannot be undone.

steps.title: Open the Credentials page (bring your own providers)
steps.content:
Now for the other direction: giving iMash your provider accounts. This is how power users control cost and capacity — your agents can run on your own OpenAI, ElevenLabs, or Deepgram plans.

  1. In the top navigation bar, click Credentials.
  2. You land on Service Providers Credentials (also reachable from the sidebar under Account → Credentials).

The page is organized into sections, each a grid of provider cards:

  • Voice Providers — text-to-speech accounts (PlayHT, RimeAI, ElevenLabs, Deepgram, Cartesia, Azure, OpenAI, IBM Watson, MiniMax).
  • LLM Providers — the agent's brain (OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, Google).
  • Custom LLM Models — any OpenAI-compatible endpoint you host or subscribe to.
  • Transcriber Providers — speech-to-text (Deepgram, Speechmatics, AssemblyAI, Groq STT).
  • Webhooks Providers — optional separate LLM keys just for post-call processing.
  • Cloud Storage — your own S3-compatible bucket for call recordings.

images.alt: Service Providers Credentials page showing the Voice Providers section with provider cards
images.caption: The Credentials page: provider cards grouped by section, starting with Voice Providers.

images.alt: Service Providers Credentials page showing the Voice Providers section with provider cards
images.caption: The Credentials page: provider cards grouped by section, starting with Voice Providers.

steps.title: Add a provider key
steps.content:
Every provider card works the same way:

  1. Find the provider's card (for example ElevenLabs under Voice Providers).
  2. Paste your key into the API Key field. Some providers ask for one extra field — PlayHT wants a User ID, Deepgram an optional API Url, Azure a Region, Twilio-style providers an account SID and token.
  3. Click Save. The button only activates once you've changed something.

After saving, the card locks: values display as hidden dots and the buttons change to Edit (re-enter a new key) and Delete (remove the credential after a confirmation). Nobody — including you — can read a saved key back from the screen.

💡 The 🔗 link icon in each card's corner opens that provider's own documentation, handy when you're hunting for where to create the key on their side.

images.alt: ElevenLabs credential card with an API Key field and Save button
images.caption: A provider card: paste your key, click Save, and the value is hidden from then on.

images.alt: ElevenLabs credential card with an API Key field and Save button
images.caption: A provider card: paste your key, click Save, and the value is hidden from then on.

steps.title: Add a Custom LLM model (optional)
steps.content:
Beyond the big-name providers, the Custom LLM Models section lets you plug in any OpenAI-compatible endpoint — DeepSeek, Together, OpenRouter, or a self-hosted vLLM server:

  1. Scroll to Custom LLM Models and click + Add Custom LLM.
  2. Fill in the form:
    • Name — the label shown in model pickers (e.g. DeepSeek V3).
    • Model code — the exact model id the endpoint expects (e.g. deepseek-chat).
    • Base URL — the endpoint root (e.g. https://api.deepseek.com/v1).
    • API key — your key for that endpoint.
    • Use for — where the model becomes selectable: the live agent, post-call webhooks, and/or the observer agent.
  3. Click save in the modal — the model appears as a card and shows up in the assistant editor's model picker.

Cards labeled Global (provided by iMash admin) are platform-shared models you can use but not edit.

images.alt: Custom LLM Models section with Add Custom LLM button and a model card showing base URL, model code, and use-for tags
images.caption: Custom LLM Models: bring any OpenAI-compatible endpoint into your model pickers.

images.alt: Custom LLM Models section with Add Custom LLM button and a model card showing base URL, model code, and use-for tags
images.caption: Custom LLM Models: bring any OpenAI-compatible endpoint into your model pickers.

steps.title: Webhooks Providers: separate keys for post-call AI (optional)
steps.content:
After each call, iMash runs LLM-powered post-call work: the summary, success evaluation, structured-data extraction, lifecycle stage, job creation, and more. The Webhooks Providers section lets you key that work separately from your live agents:

  • Leave a provider blank and post-call processing falls back to the key in your LLM Providers section above — the simple default.
  • Add a key here to route webhook work through a different account — a cheaper plan or a separate rate-limit pool so heavy post-call traffic never slows your live agents. When a provider is keyed here, iMash bills you $0 for webhooks on that provider.

images.alt: Webhooks Providers section with OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, and Google cards for separate post-call LLM keys
images.caption: Optional dedicated keys for post-call processing — blank cards fall back to your main LLM keys.

images.alt: Webhooks Providers section with OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, and Google cards for separate post-call LLM keys
images.caption: Optional dedicated keys for post-call processing — blank cards fall back to your main LLM keys.

steps.title: Cloud Storage: keep recordings in your own S3 bucket (optional)
steps.content:
By default, call recordings live in iMash's storage. The Cloud Storage section lets you point recordings at your own S3-compatible bucket instead — great for compliance, retention policies, or just owning your data:

  1. Pick your S3 Provider: Amazon S3, MinIO, Cloudflare R2, DigitalOcean Spaces, Backblaze B2, Wasabi, or Custom S3-Compatible.
  2. Fill the required fields: Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Region (e.g. us-east-1), and Bucket Name. Non-AWS providers also require the Endpoint URL (each provider shows an example format).
  3. Optionally set a Bucket Prefix (e.g. recordings/) to keep files in a folder, and turn on Force Path Style for MinIO and some S3-compatible services.
  4. Click Test Connection to verify your details, then Save. Saving re-tests the connection and automatically configures CORS on your bucket so recordings play in the browser — nothing is stored unless the test passes.
  5. Flip Enable Custom S3 Storage to ON to switch new recordings to your bucket. Optionally enable Also Store Call Data to keep transcripts and analysis results there too.
💡 The enable toggle stays locked until every required field is filled — if it won't switch on, look for the empty field.

images.alt: S3-Compatible Storage card with provider selector, access keys, region, bucket fields, and Test Connection button
images.caption: The S3-Compatible Storage card: connect your own bucket for call recordings.

images.alt: S3-Compatible Storage card with provider selector, access keys, region, bucket fields, and Test Connection button
images.caption: The S3-Compatible Storage card: connect your own bucket for call recordings.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaDescription: Generate, copy, and revoke iMash API keys for REST API access, and store your own provider credentials — TTS, LLM, transcription, custom LLM endpoints, webhook keys, and S3 recording storage.
